title: Stream of characters
description: Check if suffix matches any word in Trie

examples.explanation: Initialize StreamChecker with ['cd','f','kl']. Stream starts empty. Query 'a': stream='a', check suffixes: 'cd' (len2) vs 'a' (len1) - no match, 'f' (len1) vs 'a' - no match, 'kl' (len2) vs 'a' - no match → false. Query 'b': stream='ab', check: 'cd' vs 'ab' - no, 'f' vs 'b' - no, 'kl' vs 'ab' - no → false. Query 'c': stream='abc', check: 'cd' vs 'abc' (compare 'd' vs 'c' - mismatch), 'f' vs 'c' - no, 'kl' vs 'abc' - no → false. Query 'd': stream='abcd', check: 'cd' vs 'abcd' (compare 'd' vs 'd' match, 'c' vs 'c' match) → true found! Return true. Query 'e': stream='abcde', check: 'cd' vs 'abcde' (compare 'd' vs 'e' mismatch), 'f' vs 'e' - no, 'kl' vs 'abcde' - no → false. Query 'f': stream='abcdef', check: 'cd' vs 'abcdef' (compare 'd' vs 'f' mismatch), 'f' vs 'f' match → true found! Return true.

Initialize with ['ab','bc','abc']. Query 'a': stream='a', check: 'ab' vs 'a' - no, 'bc' vs 'a' - no, 'abc' vs 'a' - no → false. Query 'a': stream='aa', check: 'ab' vs 'aa' (compare 'b' vs 'a' mismatch), 'bc' vs 'aa' - no, 'abc' vs 'aa' - no → false. Query 'b': stream='aab', check: 'ab' vs 'aab' (compare 'b' vs 'b' match, 'a' vs 'a' match) → true! Return true. Query 'c': stream='aabc', check: 'ab' vs 'aabc' (compare 'b' vs 'c' mismatch), 'bc' vs 'aabc' (compare 'c' vs 'c' match, 'b' vs 'b' match) → true! Return true.

approaches.name: Brute Force O(n * m * k)
approaches.summary: For each query, iterate through all words in the dictionary and check if any word matches the suffix of the stream by comparing characters from the end. This is straightforward but inefficient for large streams or dictionaries.

ladders.title: Full Problem — Stream of Characters with Brute Force Suffix Matching
ladders.desc: Implement a StreamChecker class that processes a stream of characters and checks if any suffix matches words in a dictionary using brute force comparison.
ladders.explanation: This is the complete problem implementation using the brute force approach. The intuition is straightforward: for each query character added to the stream, we need to check if any word in the dictionary appears as a suffix of the current stream. Since we're using brute force, we'll simply iterate through all dictionary words for each query and compare characters from the end of the stream. The algorithm works by maintaining the stream as a string or list, and for each query, we append the character to the stream, then check each dictionary word to see if it matches the end of the stream. We compare by starting from the end of both the stream and the dictionary word, moving backward character by character. If we find a match for any word, we return true; otherwise false after checking all words. Time complexity is O(n * m * k) where n is number of queries, m is number of dictionary words, and k is average word length. Space is O(1) excluding input storage. This connects to L1 by using the suffix matching subroutine repeatedly. Common mistakes include: 1) Not handling empty streams properly, 2) Off-by-one errors when comparing indices, 3) Forgetting to reset comparison variables for each word, 4) Inefficiently rebuilding the stream string repeatedly. Real code pattern: class StreamChecker with __init__(self, words) storing dictionary, and query(self, letter) implementing the brute force check.

examples.trace: Initialize StreamChecker with ['cd','f','kl']. Stream starts empty. Query 'a': stream='a', check suffixes: 'cd' (len2) vs 'a' (len1) - no match, 'f' (len1) vs 'a' - no match, 'kl' (len2) vs 'a' - no match → false. Query 'b': stream='ab', check: 'cd' vs 'ab' - no, 'f' vs 'b' - no, 'kl' vs 'ab' - no → false. Query 'c': stream='abc', check: 'cd' vs 'abc' (compare 'd' vs 'c' - mismatch), 'f' vs 'c' - no, 'kl' vs 'abc' - no → false. Query 'd': stream='abcd', check: 'cd' vs 'abcd' (compare 'd' vs 'd' match, 'c' vs 'c' match) → true found! Return true. Query 'e': stream='abcde', check: 'cd' vs 'abcde' (compare 'd' vs 'e' mismatch), 'f' vs 'e' - no, 'kl' vs 'abcde' - no → false. Query 'f': stream='abcdef', check: 'cd' vs 'abcdef' (compare 'd' vs 'f' mismatch), 'f' vs 'f' match → true found! Return true.

Initialize with ['ab','bc','abc']. Query 'a': stream='a', check: 'ab' vs 'a' - no, 'bc' vs 'a' - no, 'abc' vs 'a' - no → false. Query 'a': stream='aa', check: 'ab' vs 'aa' (compare 'b' vs 'a' mismatch), 'bc' vs 'aa' - no, 'abc' vs 'aa' - no → false. Query 'b': stream='aab', check: 'ab' vs 'aab' (compare 'b' vs 'b' match, 'a' vs 'a' match) → true! Return true. Query 'c': stream='aabc', check: 'ab' vs 'aabc' (compare 'b' vs 'c' mismatch), 'bc' vs 'aabc' (compare 'c' vs 'c' match, 'b' vs 'b' match) → true! Return true.

ladders.title: Key Sub-routine — Brute Force Suffix Matching for a Single Word
ladders.desc: Check if a given word matches the suffix of a given string by comparing characters from the end.
ladders.explanation: This is the core matching subroutine used in the full problem. The intuition is that to check if word W appears as a suffix of string S, we need to compare the last characters of both strings, moving backward. If W is longer than S, it cannot be a suffix. We start from the end of both strings and compare character by character moving left. If all characters match and we reach the beginning of W, then W is a suffix of S. The algorithm: 1) If len(W) > len(S), return false. 2) Initialize i = len(S)-1, j = len(W)-1. 3) While j >= 0: if S[i] != W[j], return false; else decrement i and j. 4) If loop completes, return true. Time complexity is O(k) where k is length of W. Space is O(1). This feeds into L0 by being called for each dictionary word for each query. Common mistakes: 1) Starting comparison from beginning instead of end, 2) Not checking length condition first, 3) Off-by-one errors with indices, 4) Not handling empty strings properly. Real code pattern: def is_suffix(word, stream): if len(word) > len(stream): return False; for i in range(1, len(word)+1): if stream[-i] != word[-i]: return False; return True.

examples.trace: Word length=2, stream length=4. Since 2 ≤ 4, proceed. Compare last chars: stream[3]='d' vs word[1]='d' → match. Move left: stream[2]='c' vs word[0]='c' → match. Reached beginning of word → all matched. Return true.
Word length=3, stream length=2. Since 3 > 2, word cannot be suffix. Return false immediately.

ladders.title: Core Logic — Iterating Through Dictionary and Checking Suffixes
ladders.desc: Given a stream string and a dictionary of words, check if any word matches the stream's suffix by applying suffix matching to each word.
ladders.explanation: This represents the main logic loop of the brute force approach. The intuition is that to solve the full problem, we need to check every word in the dictionary against the current stream. We iterate through the dictionary list, and for each word, we use the suffix matching subroutine (from L1). If any word matches, we can return true immediately (short-circuit). If we check all words and none match, return false. The algorithm: 1) For each word in dictionary: 2) Call is_suffix(word, stream). 3) If true, return true. 4) After loop, return false. Time complexity is O(m * k) where m is number of words, k is average word length. Space is O(1). This connects to L0 by being the core check performed for each query. Common mistakes: 1) Not short-circuiting when match found (continuing to check all words unnecessarily), 2) Modifying the dictionary during iteration, 3) Not handling empty dictionary, 4) Confusing this with prefix matching. Real code pattern: def any_suffix_match(stream, words): for word in words: if is_suffix(word, stream): return True; return False.

examples.trace: Check word 'cd': is_suffix('cd','abcdef') → compare 'd' vs 'f' mismatch → false. Check word 'f': is_suffix('f','abcdef') → compare 'f' vs 'f' match → true! Return true immediately (short-circuit). Don't check 'kl'.

Check word 'xy': is_suffix('xy','abc') → length check: 2 ≤ 3, compare 'y' vs 'c' mismatch → false. Check word 'yz': is_suffix('yz','abc') → length check: 2 ≤ 3, compare 'z' vs 'c' mismatch → false. All words checked, none match → return false.

ladders.title: Building Block — Comparing Two Strings from the End
ladders.desc: Compare two strings character by character starting from their last characters moving backward.
ladders.explanation: This is a fundamental string operation needed for suffix matching. The intuition is that when checking if one string is a suffix of another, we need to compare from the end because suffixes are at the end. We compare the last character of both strings, then the second-last, and so on. If at any point characters differ, the strings don't match as suffixes. The algorithm assumes both strings have sufficient length for comparison (caller handles length check). Steps: 1) Initialize indices i = len(A)-1, j = len(B)-1. 2) While i >= 0 and j >= 0: if A[i] != B[j], return false; else decrement i and j. 3) Return true if loop completes (all compared characters matched). Time O(min(len(A), len(B))), space O(1). This connects to L1 by being the comparison part of suffix matching. Common mistakes: 1) Comparing from start instead of end, 2) Not decrementing indices correctly, 3) Continuing comparison beyond string boundaries, 4) Not handling case sensitivity if required. Real code pattern: def compare_from_end(str1, str2, chars_to_compare): for i in range(chars_to_compare): if str1[-1-i] != str2[-1-i]: return False; return True.

examples.trace: Compare last char: A[3]='d' vs B[1]='d' → match. Compare second-last: A[2]='c' vs B[0]='c' → match. Compared 2 characters, all match → return true.
Compare last: A[5]='f' vs B[2]='z' → mismatch! Return false immediately. Don't compare further.

ladders.title: Basic Operation — Accessing String Characters from the End
ladders.desc: Access characters in a string using negative indices to count from the end.
ladders.explanation: This is a fundamental programming operation needed for suffix comparisons. The intuition is that strings are typically indexed from the front (0, 1, 2...), but to work with suffixes, we need to access characters from the end. Most programming languages allow negative indices where -1 refers to the last character, -2 to the second-last, etc. For example, in string 'hello', index -1 is 'o', -2 is 'l', -3 is 'l', -4 is 'e', -5 is 'h'. The algorithm is simply using negative indexing: str[-1] gets last character, str[-2] gets second-last, etc. Time O(1), space O(1). This connects to L3 by enabling the comparison from the end. Common mistakes: 1) Using positive indices incorrectly (e.g., trying to compute len(str)-1 manually and making off-by-one errors), 2) Accessing index beyond string length (e.g., str[-6] for 'hello' causes error), 3) Not understanding that negative indices wrap around. Real code pattern: last_char = s[-1]; second_last = s[-2]; etc.

examples.trace: String 'abcdef' has indices: 0='a',1='b',2='c',3='d',4='e',5='f'. Negative indices: -1 refers to index 5 → 'f'. -2 refers to index 4 → 'e'. -3 refers to index 3 → 'd'.
'hello': -1 → 'o', -2 → 'l', -3 → 'l', -4 → 'e', -5 → 'h'. Note -5 is the first character.

ladders.title: L5: Concept Foundation — What is a Suffix?
ladders.desc: Understand the definition of a suffix: a substring that appears at the end of a string.
ladders.explanation: This is the most basic concept needed for the entire problem. A suffix of a string is any substring that starts at some position and continues to the end of the string. Formally, for string S of length n, a suffix is S[i:n] for some i from 0 to n (where S[n:n] is empty string). For example, suffixes of 'hello' are: 'hello' (i=0), 'ello' (i=1), 'llo' (i=2), 'lo' (i=3), 'o' (i=4), '' (i=5). The intuition is that we're looking for dictionary words that match any of these suffixes. This is different from a prefix (which starts at beginning) or substring (which can appear anywhere). In this problem, we specifically check if dictionary words equal complete suffixes (not partial matches). This foundational understanding connects to L4 because to check suffixes, we need to look at the end of strings. Common mistakes: 1) Confusing suffix with prefix, 2) Thinking suffix must be proper (excluding full string), 3) Not considering empty string as valid suffix. Real code pattern: Concept only—no code, but understanding that suffix checking involves comparing ends of strings.

examples.trace: Start at index 0: 'abc'. Index 1: 'bc'. Index 2: 'c'. Index 3: '' (empty string). These are all possible suffixes.
'bc' appears at the end of 'abc' (starting at index 1, ending at index 3). Compare: 'abc' ends with 'bc' → yes, it's a suffix.

approaches.name: Reverse Trie O(n * k)
approaches.summary: Insert all words from the dictionary into a trie in reverse order, then for each query, traverse the trie backwards from the end of the stream to check for matches. This optimizes suffix lookups by leveraging a trie structure.

ladders.title: Full Problem — Stream of Characters with Reverse Trie
ladders.desc: Implement a StreamChecker class that checks if any suffix of the stream matches words in a dictionary using a reverse trie.
ladders.explanation: The full problem requires implementing a data structure that efficiently checks if any suffix of an incoming character stream matches words in a given dictionary. The reverse trie approach solves this by storing all dictionary words in reverse order in a trie. When a new character arrives in the stream, we maintain a list of possible matching positions in the reverse trie. For each query character, we traverse all current positions in the trie to see if any reach an end-of-word node, indicating a complete match. The intuition is that by reversing words, we can efficiently check suffixes by traversing from the end of the stream backward through the trie. This transforms suffix matching into prefix matching in the reversed domain. The algorithm: 1) Build a reverse trie by inserting each dictionary word in reverse order. 2) For each query character, update a list of trie nodes representing possible suffix matches. 3) Check if any node reaches an end-of-word marker. Time complexity is O(n*k) where n is stream length and k is average word length, as each character may trigger multiple trie traversals. Space is O(m*k) for the trie where m is dictionary size. This connects to L1 by using the query processing subroutine. Common mistakes include not handling multiple simultaneous matches or forgetting to reset the node list. Real code pattern: class StreamChecker with __init__(words) building reverse trie and query(ch) returning boolean.

examples.trace: Initialize with reverse trie: 'dc', 'f', 'lk'. Stream: 'a' -> nodes=[root.child['a']? none], no match. 'b' -> nodes=[root.child['b']? none], no match. 'c' -> nodes=[root.child['c']? none], no match. 'd' -> nodes=[root.child['d']? node1], check node1: not end, continue. 'e' -> nodes from previous: node1.child['e']? none, also root.child['e']? none, no match. 'f' -> nodes=[root.child['f']? node2], node2 is end for 'f', match true. 'g' -> nodes=[root.child['g']? none], no match. Continue similarly: 'k' -> nodes=[root.child['k']? node3], not end. 'l' -> nodes from previous: node3.child['l']? node4, node4 is end for 'lk', match true.

Build reverse trie for words: 'ba', 'ab', 'baaa', 'baba', 'aab'. Stream processing: First 4 'a's: no matches. 'b' at position 5: check suffixes: 'b' matches 'ba' (reversed 'ab')? Actually trace: After 'a','a','a','a','b', suffixes: 'b' (matches 'ba'? no, reverse 'ab' is 'ba', need 'b' then 'a'), 'ab' (matches 'ba'? yes, reversed 'ab' is 'ba', path root->'b'->'a' end, true). Continue step-by-step: Each query updates node list and checks ends.

ladders.title: Key Sub-routine — Query Processing in Reverse Trie
ladders.desc: Process a stream character to check if any current suffix matches a word in the reverse trie.
ladders.explanation: This subroutine handles the core query operation: given a new character from the stream, determine if any suffix ending with this character forms a word in the dictionary. The intuition is to maintain a list of trie nodes representing possible partial matches from previous characters. When a new character arrives, we update this list by traversing each node to the child corresponding to the new character. If any node reaches an end-of-word marker, we have a match. Additionally, we always start a new potential match from the root. The algorithm: 1) Initialize an empty list 'nodes' to store current trie nodes. 2) For query character 'ch': a) Create a new list 'new_nodes'. b) Add root's child for 'ch' to 'new_nodes' if it exists. c) For each node in 'nodes', if node has child 'ch', add that child to 'new_nodes'. d) Check if any node in 'new_nodes' is an end-of-word node; if yes, return true. e) Update 'nodes' to 'new_nodes'. 3) Return false if no match. Time complexity per query is O(k) where k is the number of active nodes, bounded by word length. Space is O(k) for the node list. This feeds into L0 by being the main loop in StreamChecker.query(). Common mistakes include not adding the root's child or incorrectly updating the node list. Real code pattern: def query(ch): nodes = self.nodes; new_nodes = []; if root.children[ch]: new_nodes.append(root.children[ch]); for node in nodes: if ch in node.children: new_nodes.append(node.children[ch]); self.nodes = new_nodes; return any(node.is_end for node in new_nodes).

examples.input: Trie with words 'dc' (reverse of 'cd'), 'f', 'lk' (reverse of 'kl'). Current nodes = [node_d from previous]. Query char = 'e'.
examples.trace: Start: nodes = [node_d] (node for 'd' from root). New char 'e': new_nodes = []. Check root child 'e': none. For node_d: check child 'e'? node_d has children? For 'dc', after 'd' next is 'c', not 'e', so none. new_nodes remains empty. Check ends: none. Update nodes = []. Return false.

examples.input: Trie with words 'ba' (reverse of 'ab'), 'ab' (reverse of 'ba'). Current nodes = []. Query char = 'a'.
examples.trace: Start: nodes = []. New char 'a': new_nodes = []. Check root child 'a': exists? For 'ba' and 'ab', root has 'b' and 'a'? Actually 'ba' reversed gives root->'b', 'ab' reversed gives root->'a'. So root child 'a' exists, add node_a to new_nodes. No previous nodes. Check node_a.is_end? 'a' is not a word (words are 'ab' and 'ba'), so false. Update nodes = [node_a]. Return false.

ladders.title: Core Logic — Reverse Trie for Suffix Matching
ladders.desc: Use a trie with words stored in reverse to efficiently check suffix matches.
ladders.explanation: The core insight is that by storing dictionary words in reverse order in a trie, we can transform the problem of suffix matching into prefix matching. For a stream of characters, suffixes ending at the current position correspond to reversed prefixes of the reversed stream. For example, if the stream is 'abcd' and we want to check suffix 'cd', we reverse it to 'dc' and check if 'dc' is a prefix in the reverse trie. The intuition is that a trie naturally supports prefix checks, so reversing aligns suffix checks with this strength. The technique involves: 1) Building the trie: For each word in the dictionary, insert its reverse into the trie. 2) Querying: Maintain the reversed stream as we go, but efficiently by tracking nodes. This reduces the O(n^2) brute-force suffix check to O(n*k). Time to build is O(m*k) for m words of average length k. Space is O(m*k) for trie nodes. This feeds into L1 by providing the data structure for query processing. Common mistakes include not reversing correctly or misunderstanding the matching direction. Real code pattern: class TrieNode with children dict and is_end flag; insert_reverse(word): curr = root; for ch in reversed(word): if ch not in curr.children: curr.children[ch] = TrieNode(); curr = curr.children[ch]; curr.is_end = True.

examples.output: Trie with paths: 'tac' and 'tab' from root.
examples.trace: Step 1: Word 'cat' reversed is 'tac'. Insert: root->'t'->'a'->'c', mark 'c' node as end. Step 2: Word 'bat' reversed is 'tab'. Insert: root->'t'->'a'->'b', mark 'b' node as end. Result: root has child 't', which has child 'a', which has children 'c' (end) and 'b' (end).

Reverse stream suffix: 'at' reversed is 'ta'. Check in reverse trie: root->'t' exists, then 'a' exists, but 'ta' is not a word (words are 'tac' and 'tab'). So no match. Alternatively, process stream: after 'c','a','t', suffixes: 't' reversed 't'? Check trie: 't' is not end. 'at' reversed 'ta'? Not end. 'cat' reversed 'tac'? Matches 'tac' end, true for 'cat' but not 'bat'.

testCases.expected: Trie with paths: 'a' and 'b' from root
Trie with paths: 'cba' and 'dba' sharing 'cb' and 'db'
Trie with root as end if empty word allowed

ladders.title: Building Block — Trie Data Structure
ladders.desc: Implement a trie (prefix tree) for storing strings.
ladders.explanation: A trie is a tree-like data structure used to store a dynamic set of strings, where keys are usually strings. Each node represents a character, and paths from the root to nodes spell out words. The intuition is that it allows efficient prefix searches, insertions, and deletions by sharing common prefixes among words. For this problem, we use it to store reversed words. The algorithm: 1) Define a TrieNode class with children (e.g., dictionary mapping characters to nodes) and an is_end boolean flag. 2) Insert: Start at root, for each character in the word, traverse or create child nodes, mark the final node as end. 3) Search: Traverse nodes for each character; if path exists and final node is end, word is found. 4) StartsWith: Similar to search but doesn't require end marker. Time complexity for insert/search is O(k) where k is word length. Space is O(n*k) for n words. This feeds into L2 by providing the structure for reverse storage. Common mistakes include not initializing children properly or forgetting the end marker. Real code pattern: class TrieNode: def __init__(self): self.children = {}; self.is_end = False; class Trie: def __init__(self): self.root = TrieNode(); def insert(word): curr = self.root; for ch in word: if ch not in curr.children: curr.children[ch] = TrieNode(); curr = curr.children[ch]; curr.is_end = True.

examples.trace: Start: root empty. Insert 'cat': ch 'c': root has no 'c', create node_c; curr = node_c. ch 'a': node_c has no 'a', create node_a; curr = node_a. ch 't': node_a has no 't', create node_t; curr = node_t; set is_end true. Insert 'car': start at root, 'c' exists, go to node_c. 'a' exists, go to node_a. 'r': node_a has no 'r', create node_r; set is_end true.

examples.input: Search for 'cat' and 'cab' in the above trie.
examples.trace: Search 'cat': start root, 'c' exists->node_c, 'a' exists->node_a, 't' exists->node_t, node_t.is_end true, return true. Search 'cab': root->'c'->'a'->'b'? node_a has no 'b', so return false.

ladders.title: Basic Operation — String Reversal and Prefix Check
ladders.desc: Reverse a string and check if it exists as a prefix in a collection.
ladders.explanation: This operation involves two fundamental tasks: reversing a string and checking if its reverse is a prefix in a set of strings. The intuition is that reversing transforms suffix matching into prefix matching, which is simpler. For example, to check if suffix 'cd' of 'abcd' matches a word, reverse 'cd' to 'dc' and see if 'dc' is a prefix of any reversed word. The algorithm: 1) Reversal: Iterate through the string from end to start, building a new string or using slicing. 2) Prefix check: For a set of strings, iterate through each to see if the reversed string matches the beginning. Time complexity is O(k) for reversal and O(n*k) for prefix check if done naively, but optimized with a trie in higher levels. Space is O(k) for the reversed string. This feeds into L3 by being a step in building the reverse trie. Common mistakes include off-by-one errors in reversal or incorrect prefix comparison. Real code pattern: def reverse(s): return s[::-1]; def is_prefix(reversed_s, words): for word in words: if word.startswith(reversed_s): return True; return False.

examples.trace: Start: s = 'hello', length 5. Reverse: iterate indices 4 to 0: s[4]='o', s[3]='l', s[2]='l', s[1]='e', s[0]='h'. Concatenate: 'olleh'.

examples.input: Reversed string 'dc', words = ['abcd', 'bcde', 'cdef']. Check if 'dc' is a prefix of any reversed word.
examples.trace: First reverse words: 'abcd' reversed 'dcba', 'bcde' reversed 'edcb', 'cdef' reversed 'fedc'. Check prefixes: 'dcba' starts with 'dc'? Yes, 'dc' matches first two chars 'd','c'. So true. Wait, example says false? Let's correct: Actually, if words are not reversed, check 'dc' as prefix of original words? 'abcd' starts with 'dc'? No. 'bcde'? No. 'cdef'? No. So false. But for this operation, we assume words are as given, so false.

ladders.title: Concept Foundation — Strings and Characters
ladders.desc: Understand basic string operations: accessing characters and building strings.
ladders.explanation: This foundation covers the basics of strings as sequences of characters. In programming, a string is an array of characters, and we can access individual characters by index, iterate through them, and concatenate to form new strings. The intuition is that strings are fundamental for text processing, and operations like reversal or prefix checks rely on these basics. Key concepts: 1) Characters: Single letters or symbols, e.g., 'a', 'b'. 2) String indexing: Position starts at 0, so for s = 'hello', s[0]='h', s[4]='o'. 3) Iteration: Loop through each character using for ch in s. 4) Concatenation: Combine strings with + operator. 5) Slicing: Extract substrings, e.g., s[1:3]='el'. Time complexity for access is O(1), for concatenation O(n) where n is length. Space depends on string size. This feeds into L4 by enabling reversal and prefix operations. Common mistakes include index out of bounds or misunderstanding string immutability. Real code pattern: s = 'hello'; first_char = s[0]; for ch in s: print(ch); new_s = s + ' world'; substring = s[1:4].

examples.trace: s[0] = 'c', s[1] = 'a', s[2] = 't'. Iteration: for ch in s: first iteration ch='c', second ch='a', third ch='t'.
Start with empty string result = ''. Add 'd': result = 'd'. Add 'o': result = 'do'. Add 'g': result = 'dog'.

approaches.name: Rolling Hash with HashSet O(n * k)
approaches.summary: Precompute hash values for all suffixes of words in the dictionary and store them in a hash set, then for each query, compute the hash of the current stream suffix and check for existence. This uses hashing for fast lookups but may have collision issues.

ladders.title: Full Problem — Stream of Characters with Rolling Hash
ladders.desc: Implement a StreamChecker that processes a stream of characters and checks if any suffix of the stream matches a word in a dictionary using rolling hash with hash set.
ladders.explanation: This problem requires efficiently checking if any suffix of an ever-growing character stream matches any word in a given dictionary. The rolling hash approach precomputes hash values for all suffixes of dictionary words and stores them in a hash set for O(1) lookups. For each query, we maintain the current stream as a string, compute the hash of its suffix (from the end backwards up to the maximum word length), and check if that hash exists in our precomputed set. The intuition is that by hashing suffixes, we can quickly compare the stream's ending against all dictionary words without scanning the entire dictionary each time. We use polynomial rolling hash with a large prime base and modulus to minimize collisions. The algorithm: 1. Preprocess: For each word in dictionary, compute hash for all its suffixes and add to hash set. Track max word length. 2. Initialize: Create empty stream string. 3. Query: Append character to stream. If stream length exceeds max word length, remove first character. Compute rolling hash from end of stream backwards up to max word length, checking each suffix hash against the set. Return true if any match found. Time: O(n*k) for preprocessing where n is number of words, k is average length. O(k) per query. Space: O(m*k) where m is total characters in dictionary. Connection to L1: This uses the suffix hash computation from L1 as its core operation. Common mistakes: Not handling stream truncation when it exceeds max word length, incorrect hash computation for suffixes, hash collisions. Real code pattern: class StreamChecker: def __init__(self, words): self.max_len = max(len(w) for w in words); self.hash_set = set(); for w in words: h = 0; for i in range(len(w)): h = (h * base + ord(w[i])) % mod; self.hash_set.add(h); for i in range(1, len(w)): h = (h - ord(w[i-1]) * pow(base, len(w)-i, mod)) % mod; self.hash_set.add(h); self.stream = ''; def query(self, letter): self.stream += letter; if len(self.stream) > self.max_len: self.stream = self.stream[1:]; h = 0; for i in range(len(self.stream)-1, -1, -1): h = (h * base + ord(self.stream[i])) % mod; if h in self.hash_set: return True; return False;

examples.trace: Initialize: words=['cd','f','kl'], max_len=2, hash_set={hash('cd'), hash('d'), hash('f'), hash('kl'), hash('l')}. Query 1: stream='a', suffix hashes: hash('a') not in set → false. Query 2: stream='ab', suffix hashes: hash('ab'), hash('b') not in set → false. Query 3: stream='abc', truncate to 'bc', suffix hashes: hash('bc'), hash('c') not in set → false. Query 4: stream='bcd', truncate to 'cd', suffix hashes: hash('cd') in set → true. Query 5: stream='cde', truncate to 'de', suffix hashes: hash('de'), hash('e') not in set → false. Query 6: stream='def', truncate to 'ef', suffix hashes: hash('ef'), hash('f') in set → true. Query 7: stream='efg', truncate to 'fg', suffix hashes: hash('fg'), hash('g') not in set → false. Query 8: stream='fgh', truncate to 'gh', suffix hashes: hash('gh'), hash('h') not in set → false. Query 9: stream='ghi', truncate to 'hi', suffix hashes: hash('hi'), hash('i') not in set → false. Query 10: stream='hij', truncate to 'ij', suffix hashes: hash('ij'), hash('j') not in set → false. Query 11: stream='ijk', truncate to 'jk', suffix hashes: hash('jk'), hash('k') not in set → false. Query 12: stream='jkl', truncate to 'kl', suffix hashes: hash('kl') in set → true.

Initialize: words=['ab','bc','abc'], max_len=3, hash_set={hash('ab'), hash('b'), hash('bc'), hash('c'), hash('abc'), hash('bc'), hash('c')}. Query 1: stream='a', suffix hashes: hash('a') not in set → false. Query 2: stream='ab', suffix hashes: hash('ab') in set → true. Query 3: stream='abc', suffix hashes: hash('abc') in set → true (also hash('bc') and hash('c') are in set).

ladders.title: Key Sub-routine — Compute All Suffix Hashes for a Word
ladders.desc: Given a word, compute rolling hash values for all its suffixes and store them in a set.
ladders.explanation: This sub-routine is crucial because it precomputes all possible suffix hashes that need to be matched against the stream. The intuition is that by storing hash values of all suffixes (e.g., for 'abc', store hashes of 'abc', 'bc', 'c'), we can later check if any suffix of the stream matches any dictionary word suffix in O(1) time. We use polynomial rolling hash: hash(s) = (s[0]*p^(n-1) + s[1]*p^(n-2) + ... + s[n-1]*p^0) mod m, where p is a prime base (e.g., 31) and m is a large prime modulus (e.g., 10^9+7). To compute all suffixes efficiently, we first compute the full word hash, then iteratively remove the first character's contribution using the formula: new_hash = (old_hash - char_val * p^(k-1)) mod m, where k is the current suffix length. Step-by-step: 1. Initialize empty set. 2. Compute full word hash using rolling hash. 3. Add this hash to set. 4. For i from 1 to len(word)-1: compute hash of suffix starting at i by subtracting contribution of word[i-1] and adjusting power. 5. Return the set. Time: O(k) where k is word length. Space: O(k) for storing hashes. Connection to L0: This is called for each dictionary word during preprocessing. Common mistakes: Incorrect power calculation, not using modulo properly, missing the full word hash. Real code pattern: def compute_suffix_hashes(word): hashes = set(); n = len(word); h = 0; for ch in word: h = (h * BASE + ord(ch)) % MOD; hashes.add(h); power = pow(BASE, n-1, MOD); for i in range(1, n): h = (h - ord(word[i-1]) * power) % MOD; h = (h * BASE + ord(word[i])) % MOD; # Adjust for new suffix length if needed, but simpler: recompute from scratch for each suffix or use above subtraction method. Actually better: recompute each suffix hash independently for clarity: for start in range(n): h = 0; for j in range(start, n): h = (h * BASE + ord(word[j])) % MOD; hashes.add(h); return hashes;

examples.trace: Compute hash for 'ab': h=0; h=(0*31+97)=97; h=(97*31+98)=3105; add 3105. Compute hash for 'b': h=0; h=(0*31+98)=98; add 98. Result set = {3105, 98}.

ladders.title: Core Logic — Rolling Hash Computation for a String
ladders.desc: Compute the polynomial rolling hash value of a given string using a base and modulus.
ladders.explanation: Rolling hash is a technique to compute a hash value for a string that allows efficient updates when characters are added or removed. The core insight is to treat the string as a polynomial where each character's ASCII value is a coefficient, and the base is a prime number. The hash is computed as: H(s) = (s[0]*p^(n-1) + s[1]*p^(n-2) + ... + s[n-1]*p^0) mod m, where p is the base, m is the modulus, and n is string length. This formulation allows us to update the hash when a new character is appended: new_hash = (old_hash * p + new_char) mod m. Similarly, to remove the first character: new_hash = (old_hash - first_char * p^(n-1)) mod m. The intuition is that by using a large prime base and modulus, we minimize collisions while enabling O(1) updates. Step-by-step for computing hash from scratch: 1. Initialize hash = 0. 2. For each character c in string: hash = (hash * p + ord(c)) % m. 3. Return hash. Time: O(n) for a string of length n. Space: O(1). Connection to L1: This is used to compute each suffix hash. Common mistakes: Forgetting modulo operation, using small base leading to collisions, integer overflow. Real code pattern: def rolling_hash(s, base=31, mod=10**9+7): h = 0; for ch in s: h = (h * base + ord(ch)) % mod; return h;

ladders.title: Building Block — Modular Arithmetic for Hashing
ladders.desc: Perform modulo operations to prevent integer overflow and manage hash values within a fixed range.
ladders.explanation: Modular arithmetic is essential in rolling hash to keep hash values within a manageable range and prevent integer overflow. The core idea is to use the modulo operation (%) after each arithmetic step to ensure the hash value stays between 0 and modulus-1. This works because (a * b) mod m = ((a mod m) * (b mod m)) mod m and (a + b) mod m = ((a mod m) + (b mod m)) mod m. In rolling hash, when we compute hash = (hash * base + char_code) % mod, we're applying these properties. The intuition is that by taking mod at each step, we maintain a consistent hash value that can be compared, while avoiding large numbers that could slow down computations or cause overflow. Step-by-step for a simple example: compute (97*31 + 98) mod 1000. First, 97*31 = 3007, then 3007 mod 1000 = 7, then 7+98=105, then 105 mod 1000 = 105. Alternatively, compute directly: (3007+98)=3105, 3105 mod 1000 = 105. Both give same result due to modular arithmetic properties. Time: O(1) per operation. Space: O(1). Connection to L2: This is used in every step of rolling hash computation. Common mistakes: Not applying modulo consistently, using modulus that is not prime (increasing collision chance), misunderstanding negative results in modulo (use (x % m + m) % m in some languages). Real code pattern: In Python, % handles positive mod directly. Example: val = (previous * base + ord(ch)) % modulus.

ladders.title: Basic Operation — ASCII Value to Integer Conversion
ladders.desc: Convert a character to its ASCII integer value for use in hash computations.
ladders.explanation: In rolling hash, we need to convert each character to a numerical value to use in polynomial computation. ASCII (American Standard Code for Information Interchange) provides a standard mapping where each character is assigned an integer between 0 and 127. For example, 'a' maps to 97, 'b' to 98, 'A' to 65, '0' to 48. The intuition is that by converting characters to integers, we can perform arithmetic operations like addition and multiplication to compute a hash. This conversion is straightforward in most programming languages using functions like ord() in Python or casting in other languages. Step-by-step: Given a character 'c', look up its ASCII value. For hash computation, we use this value directly. For example, for string 'ab', we use 97 and 98. Time: O(1) per character. Space: O(1). Connection to L3: This integer value is used in the modular arithmetic operations. Common mistakes: Assuming case-insensitive hashing without normalization, not handling non-ASCII characters (in this problem, we assume lowercase English letters, so ASCII is fine). Real code pattern: ascii_val = ord('a')  # returns 97.

examples.trace: The ASCII code for lowercase 'a' is 97. So output is 97.
The ASCII code for digit '0' is 48. So output is 48.

ladders.title: L5: Concept Foundation — Strings and Characters
ladders.desc: Understand basic string operations: accessing characters and building strings.
ladders.explanation: A hash function is a fundamental concept in computer science that takes an input (like a string) and returns a fixed-size value called a hash code. The primary purpose is to enable fast data retrieval by using this hash code as an index in a data structure like a hash set or hash map. For example, when storing words in a set, instead of comparing the entire word each time, we compute its hash and check if that hash exists. The intuition is that hashing reduces comparison time from O(n) to O(1) on average. Key properties: 1. Deterministic: same input always produces same hash. 2. Fast to compute. 3. Uniform distribution: different inputs should map to different hashes to minimize collisions. In the context of our problem, we use a rolling hash function that computes a polynomial-based hash for strings. This allows us to quickly check if a suffix matches by comparing hash values rather than the full strings. Step-by-step for a simple hash: input 'cat', compute hash by summing ASCII values: 99+97+116=312. This is a naive hash function. Time: O(n) for string length n. Space: O(1). Connection to L4: The hash function uses ASCII values as input. Common mistakes: Using a hash function with many collisions, not handling empty strings. Real code pattern: def simple_hash(s): return sum(ord(c) for c in s).

examples.input: Compute simple hash for 'ab' by summing ASCII
examples.trace: ASCII of 'a' is 97, 'b' is 98. Sum = 97+98 = 195. So hash is 195.

examples.input: Compute simple hash for 'ba' by summing ASCII
examples.trace: ASCII of 'b' is 98, 'a' is 97. Sum = 98+97 = 195. This shows collision: 'ab' and 'ba' have same hash.
